lessons.title: AWS Overview
lessons.overview: Learn the basics of Amazon Web Services: what it is, when it started, why it dominates the cloud industry, and how the pay-as-you-go model works.
lessons.overviewVi: Tìm hiểu những kiến thức nền tảng về Amazon Web Services: AWS là gì, ra đời khi nào, tại sao dẫn đầu ngành điện toán đám mây, và mô hình trả tiền theo sử dụng hoạt động ra sao.

sections.label: Overview
sections.heading: What is AWS?
sections.contentHtml:
AWS stands for Amazon Web Services — a cloud platform owned by Amazon Incorporated, the world's largest online retailer.

Important distinction: amazon.com (the e-commerce retail site) is completely separate from AWS (the cloud platform). Two distinct business units.

The term web services refers to software or resources you can access over the Internet. AWS lets you use remote computing resources to:

  • Store and retrieve files and large datasets
  • Host and run websites and applications
  • Manage virtual machines (VMs)
  • Process complex machine learning algorithms
  • Handle databases, messaging, analytics, and hundreds more tasks
sections.contentVi: AWS (Amazon Web Services) là nền tảng đám mây do Amazon Inc. sở hữu — hoàn toàn tách biệt với website bán lẻ amazon.com. Đây là hai đơn vị kinh doanh độc lập, không liên quan nhau. Thuật ngữ 'web services' chỉ các phần mềm hoặc tài nguyên có thể truy cập qua Internet. Với AWS, bạn có thể: lưu trữ và truy xuất file cùng tập dữ liệu lớn, lưu trữ và chạy website và ứng dụng, quản lý máy ảo (VM), xử lý các thuật toán học máy phức tạp, và xử lý cơ sở dữ liệu, nhắn tin, phân tích cùng hàng trăm tác vụ khác.

sections.label: Core Concept
sections.heading: What is a Cloud Service Provider?
sections.contentHtml:
AWS is a cloud service provider — a third-party company that provides cloud-based computing resources to enterprises, startups, and individuals. Instead of buying and maintaining physical servers, you rent virtual servers you access online at a fraction of the cost.

Car rental analogy: If you're visiting a city for one week, it makes no sense to buy a car. You simply rent one and pay only for the days you use it. AWS works the same way — rent computing resources, pay only for what you use.
Virtual serversAmazon EC2
StorageAmazon S3, EBS, EFS
DatabaseAmazon RDS, DynamoDB
NetworkingAmazon VPC, Route 53, CloudFront

Some organizations adopt a multi-cloud strategy, using two or more cloud providers simultaneously — such as AWS, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud Platform, Alibaba Cloud, or IBM Cloud.
sections.contentVi: AWS là nhà cung cấp dịch vụ đám mây — công ty bên thứ ba cho thuê tài nguyên điện toán qua Internet, thay vì buộc bạn mua và bảo trì máy chủ vật lý. Bạn thuê máy ảo (virtual server) và chỉ trả tiền cho phần thực sự sử dụng. Ví von dễ nhớ: đến một thành phố chỉ một tuần thì thuê xe rẻ hơn mua xe — AWS cũng vậy. Các dịch vụ nền tảng: máy ảo (Amazon EC2), lưu trữ (S3, EBS, EFS), cơ sở dữ liệu (RDS, DynamoDB), mạng (VPC, Route 53, CloudFront). Một số tổ chức áp dụng chiến lược multi-cloud — dùng đồng thời từ hai nhà cung cấp đám mây trở lên như AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, Alibaba Cloud hoặc IBM Cloud.

sections.label: History
sections.heading: AWS Timeline
sections.contentHtml:
2004AWS was an internal Amazon department — a collection of web APIs for the amazon.com catalog, used for internal processing only, not a public service
2006AWS officially launched as a public cloud service provider. Amazon S3 was the first public service, followed by Amazon SQS in the same year
Today200+ fully-featured services available globally; millions of customers in 190+ countries; compute, storage, database, analytics, AI/ML, security, and more
Common exam trap: AWS went public in 2006, NOT 2004. In 2004 it was still an internal department.
sections.contentVi: Năm 2004 — AWS chỉ là bộ phận nội bộ của Amazon, gồm các web API để truy cập danh mục amazon.com, dùng cho xử lý nội bộ, chưa phải dịch vụ công cộng. Năm 2006 — AWS chính thức ra mắt dịch vụ đám mây công cộng: Amazon S3 là dịch vụ đầu tiên, tiếp theo là Amazon SQS trong cùng năm. Ngày nay — hơn 200 dịch vụ đầy đủ tính năng, hàng triệu khách hàng tại hơn 190 quốc gia, bao gồm điện toán, lưu trữ, cơ sở dữ liệu, phân tích, AI/ML, bảo mật và nhiều hơn nữa. Bẫy thi hay gặp: AWS ra mắt công khai năm 2006, KHÔNG phải 2004 — năm 2004 vẫn là bộ phận nội bộ.

sections.label: Exam Focus
sections.heading: Key Facts for the SAA Exam
sections.contentHtml:
• AWS launched publicly in 2006 (2004 = internal Amazon department only)
  • Amazon S3 was the very first publicly released AWS service
  • AWS is classified primarily as IaaS, but also offers PaaS and SaaS products
  • Multi-cloud = using 2+ public cloud providers simultaneously
  • Hybrid cloud ≠ Multi-cloud: Hybrid cloud = on-premises infrastructure + public cloud
  • Pay-as-you-go: customers pay only for resources they actually consume
Remember: The exam often tests the 2004 vs 2006 date distinction and the multi-cloud vs hybrid cloud difference.
sections.contentVi: Các điểm quan trọng cần nhớ cho kỳ thi SAA: (1) AWS ra mắt công khai năm 2006, không phải 2004 — năm 2004 chỉ là bộ phận nội bộ Amazon. (2) Amazon S3 là dịch vụ công khai đầu tiên của AWS. (3) AWS được phân loại chủ yếu là IaaS, nhưng cũng cung cấp PaaS và SaaS. (4) Multi-cloud = dùng từ 2 nhà cung cấp đám mây công cộng trở lên đồng thời. (5) Phân biệt quan trọng: Multi-cloud ≠ Hybrid cloud — Hybrid cloud = hạ tầng on-premises kết hợp với đám mây công cộng. (6) Pay-as-you-go: chỉ trả tiền cho tài nguyên thực sự sử dụng. Đề thi thường khai thác nhầm lẫn giữa ngày 2004/2006 và giữa multi-cloud/hybrid cloud.

summary: AWS = Amazon Web Services, the world's leading cloud provider — completely separate from amazon.com retail
Launched publicly in 2006 with S3 (first service) and SQS — in 2004 it was still an internal Amazon department
Pay-as-you-go model: rent computing resources on demand instead of purchasing physical hardware
Multi-cloud ≠ Hybrid cloud: Multi-cloud = 2+ public cloud providers; Hybrid = on-premises + public cloud
AWS is primarily IaaS, also offering PaaS (e.g., Elastic Beanstalk) and SaaS (e.g., Amazon Chime)
summaryVi: AWS = Amazon Web Services, nhà cung cấp đám mây hàng đầu thế giới — tách biệt hoàn toàn với bán lẻ amazon.com
Ra mắt công khai năm 2006 với S3 (dịch vụ đầu tiên) và SQS — năm 2004 vẫn là bộ phận nội bộ Amazon
Mô hình pay-as-you-go: thuê tài nguyên điện toán theo nhu cầu, không cần mua phần cứng vật lý
Multi-cloud ≠ Hybrid cloud: Multi-cloud = 2+ nhà cung cấp đám mây công cộng; Hybrid = on-premises + đám mây
AWS chủ yếu là IaaS, cũng cung cấp PaaS (vd. Elastic Beanstalk) và SaaS (vd. Amazon Chime)

quiz.question: A startup wants to launch a new web application without purchasing physical servers. They need to start small and scale as their user base grows, paying only for what they use. Which AWS characteristic BEST addresses this requirement?
options: AWS provides dedicated hardware that is reserved exclusively for each customer
AWS operates on a pay-as-you-go model, allowing on-demand access to computing resources without upfront hardware investment
AWS requires a minimum 1-year commitment before provisioning any resources
AWS automatically purchases and manages physical servers on behalf of customers
quiz.explanation: The pay-as-you-go model is the core AWS pricing principle — you rent computing resources on demand and pay only for what you consume, eliminating the need to purchase and maintain physical hardware. AWS does not require minimum commitments for on-demand usage, and customers share underlying infrastructure rather than getting dedicated hardware by default.

quiz.question: A solutions architect is designing a system where the company hosts its core database on-premises in their own data center while using AWS for web tier and application tier workloads. How should this architecture be classified?
options: Multi-cloud architecture, because workloads are distributed across different environments
Hybrid cloud architecture, because it combines on-premises infrastructure with a public cloud provider
SaaS architecture, because AWS manages the application layer on behalf of the customer
Multi-cloud architecture, because AWS and on-premises are treated as two separate cloud providers
quiz.explanation: Hybrid cloud specifically means combining on-premises (private) infrastructure with a public cloud provider such as AWS. Multi-cloud refers to using two or more public cloud providers (e.g., AWS and Azure) — on-premises data centers do not qualify as a public cloud provider, so this scenario is hybrid, not multi-cloud.

quiz.question: A company currently uses AWS for production workloads and is evaluating adding Google Cloud Platform (GCP) to run their analytics pipelines. What type of cloud strategy would this represent?
options: Hybrid cloud, because two separate technology environments are being used
Private cloud, because the company retains control over its own analytics data
Multi-cloud, because the company would be leveraging two distinct public cloud providers
On-premises, because the analytics pipeline runs on infrastructure the company procures
quiz.explanation: Multi-cloud means using two or more public cloud providers — in this case AWS and GCP. Hybrid cloud is specifically the combination of on-premises infrastructure with a public cloud, which is not what is described here. The company is not running on-premises infrastructure in this scenario.

quiz.question: A company's development team wants to deploy a web application without managing the underlying operating system, patching, or runtime configuration. They want AWS to handle that layer. Which AWS service model MOST closely matches this requirement?
options: IaaS, such as Amazon EC2, where the customer manages the OS and above
PaaS, such as AWS Elastic Beanstalk, where AWS manages the infrastructure and runtime while the developer focuses on the application
SaaS, such as Amazon Chime, where AWS delivers a fully managed end-user application
On-premises hosting with AWS Direct Connect for low-latency connectivity
quiz.explanation: PaaS (Platform as a Service), exemplified by AWS Elastic Beanstalk, abstracts away OS management, patching, and runtime configuration so developers can focus on deploying their application code. IaaS (like EC2) still requires the customer to manage the OS and above. SaaS delivers a finished application to end users, not a deployment platform for custom code.

quiz.question: An enterprise team uses Amazon Chime for internal video conferencing and messaging. Under which cloud service model does Amazon Chime fall?
options: IaaS, because it runs on AWS compute infrastructure
PaaS, because developers can extend it with custom integrations
SaaS, because it is a fully managed, ready-to-use application delivered over the internet
On-premises software, because the client application is installed on employee devices
quiz.explanation: Amazon Chime is a SaaS (Software as a Service) offering — users consume it as a finished application without managing any underlying infrastructure, platform, or runtime. The client-side installation on a device does not change the cloud model; the service itself is fully managed by AWS.

quiz.question: A solutions architect needs to explain the AWS shared responsibility model and service types to a new team member. Which TWO statements correctly describe the AWS cloud service portfolio? (Choose TWO)
options: Amazon EC2 is an IaaS offering where AWS manages the physical hardware and the customer manages the operating system and above
AWS Elastic Beanstalk is a SaaS offering because it delivers a finished application to end users
AWS is primarily an IaaS provider but also offers PaaS and SaaS services
Amazon Chime is a PaaS offering because it runs on AWS-managed infrastructure
In SaaS, the customer is responsible for patching the underlying operating system
quiz.explanation: EC2 is a classic IaaS example where AWS owns the physical servers but customers manage the OS, applications, and data. AWS is predominantly IaaS but extends into PaaS (Elastic Beanstalk) and SaaS (Chime). Elastic Beanstalk is PaaS, not SaaS — it is a deployment platform, not an end-user application. Chime is SaaS. In SaaS, the cloud provider manages everything including the OS.

quiz.question: A financial services firm runs critical workloads on AWS and is considering adding Microsoft Azure to handle disaster recovery. Their production servers remain entirely in public clouds with no on-premises infrastructure. Which term BEST describes their intended architecture?
options: Hybrid cloud, because two different technology stacks are in use
Multi-cloud, because they are utilizing two separate public cloud providers
Private cloud, because sensitive financial data requires controlled environments
Hybrid cloud, because disaster recovery implies a secondary, non-primary environment
quiz.explanation: Multi-cloud is defined as using two or more public cloud providers — AWS and Azure both qualify as public clouds, making this a multi-cloud strategy. Hybrid cloud requires at least one on-premises (private) component, which this firm does not have. The presence of a DR site does not change the classification.

quiz.question: A company is migrating from a traditional data center where they own and maintain physical servers. After migration, they plan to run all workloads on Amazon EC2. What is the MOST significant operational change they should plan for?
options: They must now purchase EC2 Reserved Instances upfront before provisioning any servers
They will shift from capital expenditure for hardware to operational expenditure based on actual consumption
They must replicate their on-premises network topology exactly within AWS VPC
They will need to physically rack and cable their own servers inside an AWS data center
quiz.explanation: The fundamental shift in moving to AWS is from CapEx (purchasing physical hardware) to OpEx (paying for resources as consumed). AWS's pay-as-you-go model eliminates upfront hardware investment. Reserved Instances are an optional cost optimization, not a requirement. AWS manages all physical infrastructure — customers never touch hardware in AWS data centers.

quiz.question: A company wants to reduce costs by avoiding idle server capacity during off-peak hours. They currently own physical servers that run at 20% utilization on weekends. Which AWS characteristic would MOST directly solve this problem?
options: AWS global infrastructure, which distributes load across multiple regions automatically
AWS Availability Zones, which provide redundancy within a region
On-demand provisioning with pay-as-you-go pricing, allowing resources to be terminated when not needed
AWS Trusted Advisor, which recommends cost optimizations for existing resources
quiz.explanation: The root problem is paying for idle physical hardware. AWS's on-demand model lets you provision resources only when needed and terminate them when idle — you pay only for actual usage. AWS global infrastructure and Availability Zones address availability and fault tolerance, not idle-capacity cost. Trusted Advisor helps optimize already-running AWS resources, not the physical hardware problem.

quiz.question: A CTO is comparing cloud deployment models for a new project. The project will use AWS for compute and storage, while keeping the HR system on company-owned servers in the corporate data center due to compliance requirements. Which TWO statements correctly describe this architecture? (Choose TWO)
options: This is a multi-cloud architecture because AWS and on-premises represent two separate environments
This is a hybrid cloud architecture because it combines a public cloud with on-premises infrastructure
Multi-cloud and hybrid cloud are the same concept and can be used interchangeably
The on-premises HR system qualifies as a private cloud component in this hybrid model
This architecture would be classified as multi-cloud only if a second public cloud provider is added
quiz.explanation: Hybrid cloud = on-premises + public cloud, which is exactly this scenario. Multi-cloud specifically requires two or more public cloud providers — adding Azure or GCP would make it multi-cloud, but on-premises infrastructure does not count as a public cloud. Multi-cloud and hybrid cloud are distinct, non-interchangeable terms.

quiz.question: An organization is planning a large-scale event and expects a 10x spike in traffic for 48 hours, after which traffic returns to normal. They currently host on-premises servers sized for peak load, leaving them mostly idle the rest of the year. Which AWS benefit would provide the MOST cost-effective solution for this pattern?
options: AWS Reserved Instances, which provide discounted rates for consistent long-term workloads
AWS Dedicated Hosts, which give the organization exclusive physical server access
On-demand resource provisioning, scaling up for the event and scaling down afterward to match actual consumption
AWS Outposts, which extends AWS infrastructure into the on-premises data center
quiz.explanation: On-demand provisioning is the ideal model for bursty, predictable-but-temporary workloads — spin up capacity for 48 hours, pay only for that window, then terminate. Reserved Instances optimize cost for steady, long-running workloads, not short spikes. Dedicated Hosts add cost for exclusive hardware access and are not cost-effective for temporary bursts. Outposts keeps infrastructure on-premises, which is the current problem.

quiz.question: A team lead tells a junior architect: 'Since AWS is owned by Amazon, our e-commerce orders placed on amazon.com automatically integrate with our AWS workloads.' How should the architect respond?
options: This is correct — Amazon.com retail and AWS share the same backend systems by default
This is incorrect — AWS is a separate cloud computing division from Amazon.com retail and there is no automatic integration between the two
This is partially correct — order data is shared, but payment processing remains separate
This is correct only if the team uses Amazon DynamoDB, which powers both platforms
quiz.explanation: AWS (Amazon Web Services) is a completely separate business unit from Amazon.com retail. AWS is a public cloud provider that any company can use — it does not have any built-in or automatic connection to Amazon's retail platform. Any integration would require explicit, custom development.

quiz.question: A company wants to give its development teams the ability to provision virtual machines, storage, and networking without waiting for a physical hardware procurement process. Which cloud service model does AWS EC2, S3, and VPC collectively represent?
options: SaaS, because these services are delivered over the internet
PaaS, because developers do not need to write infrastructure code
IaaS, because AWS provides virtualized compute, storage, and networking resources that customers control
On-premises, because VPC simulates a private network identical to a data center
quiz.explanation: IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service) provides virtualized fundamental computing resources — compute (EC2), storage (S3), and networking (VPC) — over the internet, with the customer responsible for the OS and everything above. SaaS delivers finished applications. PaaS abstracts the OS and runtime. VPC being network-like does not make it on-premises.

quiz.question: A solutions architect is helping a company decide between using AWS Elastic Beanstalk versus Amazon EC2 directly. Which TWO statements correctly differentiate these options? (Choose TWO)
options: Elastic Beanstalk is a PaaS offering that handles OS patching, runtime management, and deployment, reducing operational overhead
EC2 is a SaaS offering because it delivers applications directly to end users
EC2 is an IaaS offering where the customer retains control and responsibility for the operating system and application stack
Elastic Beanstalk removes all developer control, including the ability to access the underlying EC2 instances
Both EC2 and Elastic Beanstalk are classified as IaaS because they both use virtual machines
quiz.explanation: Elastic Beanstalk is PaaS — it manages the platform layer (OS, runtime, load balancing) so developers focus on code, with LEAST operational overhead. EC2 is IaaS — developers get a bare virtual machine and are responsible for everything from the OS upward. EC2 is not SaaS. Elastic Beanstalk does allow SSH access to underlying instances. They are not both IaaS.

quiz.question: A retail company experiences highly variable traffic — 10x spikes during holiday seasons and very low traffic during slow months. Their current on-premises setup requires them to maintain hardware for peak capacity year-round. Which AWS advantage would provide the HIGHEST cost savings for this pattern?
options: AWS provides free tier resources that cover all workloads during low-traffic months
AWS allows resources to be provisioned on demand and released when no longer needed, so the company pays only for actual consumption rather than peak capacity
AWS Reserved Instances guarantee the lowest possible price for all workload types
AWS automatically right-sizes instances based on traffic patterns at no additional cost
quiz.explanation: The pay-as-you-go, on-demand model directly solves the over-provisioning problem — during low months, resources can be scaled down or terminated, and during peaks they scale up, matching cost to actual usage. Free tier has very limited scope. Reserved Instances are best for steady workloads, not highly variable ones. Auto-scaling is a separate feature that does not change the billing model by itself.

quiz.question: A company currently runs workloads on AWS and on-premises. The CIO proposes adding Alibaba Cloud to support operations in China due to regulatory requirements. After this addition, how should the overall cloud strategy be described?
options: Hybrid cloud only, because on-premises infrastructure is still part of the architecture
Multi-cloud only, because two public cloud providers are now in use
Both hybrid and multi-cloud, because the architecture includes on-premises infrastructure and multiple public cloud providers
Private cloud, because regulatory requirements necessitate controlled data environments
quiz.explanation: These two terms are not mutually exclusive. Hybrid cloud describes the on-premises + public cloud combination, while multi-cloud describes the use of AWS + Alibaba Cloud (two public providers). An architecture can be simultaneously hybrid and multi-cloud. Regulatory requirements do not automatically make something a private cloud.

quiz.question: A developer is evaluating whether to use AWS Elastic Beanstalk or manage EC2 instances directly for deploying a Node.js application. The team has limited DevOps expertise and wants to minimize time spent on infrastructure management. Which choice represents the LEAST operational overhead?
options: EC2 with a custom AMI, because it gives the team full control over every configuration detail
EC2 with AWS Systems Manager, because patching can be automated through patch baselines
AWS Elastic Beanstalk, because it handles OS patching, load balancing, and deployment automation as a PaaS offering
EC2 Spot Instances, because they provide the lowest cost and least management complexity
quiz.explanation: Elastic Beanstalk is a PaaS that abstracts OS management, patching, load balancer configuration, and deployment pipelines — the developer only needs to upload application code. EC2-based approaches (even with Systems Manager) require significantly more operational work. Spot Instances reduce cost but do not reduce operational overhead for OS and platform management.

quiz.question: A cloud architecture team is reviewing the differences between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS as they plan a migration. Which THREE statements are accurate descriptions of these service models as represented by AWS offerings? (Choose THREE)
options: Amazon EC2 is IaaS — the customer manages the OS, middleware, and application; AWS manages the physical hardware
AWS Elastic Beanstalk is PaaS — AWS manages the platform and runtime, and the customer deploys application code
Amazon Chime is IaaS — customers must configure the underlying compute before using the service
In SaaS, the cloud provider manages everything including the application, runtime, OS, and hardware
In IaaS, the cloud provider is responsible for managing the customer's application code and business logic
quiz.explanation: EC2 as IaaS and Elastic Beanstalk as PaaS are the canonical AWS examples. In SaaS (like Amazon Chime), the provider manages the full stack — users simply use the application. Amazon Chime is SaaS, not IaaS — no configuration of underlying compute is required. In IaaS, the provider only manages physical infrastructure; application code responsibility stays with the customer.

quiz.question: A startup CFO is concerned about the large capital expenditure required to build out a data center. The CTO suggests migrating all infrastructure to AWS. What is the PRIMARY financial model advantage of AWS that the CTO should highlight?
options: AWS provides permanent free infrastructure for qualifying startups, eliminating all cloud costs
AWS converts capital expenditure into operational expenditure by charging only for resources consumed, eliminating upfront hardware investment
AWS offers financing options that allow startups to purchase data center equipment at lower interest rates
AWS charges a flat monthly fee regardless of usage, making budget forecasting completely predictable
quiz.explanation: The shift from CapEx (large upfront hardware purchase) to OpEx (pay-per-use operational expense) is the primary financial argument for cloud adoption. AWS does not offer free infrastructure beyond the limited free tier, does not finance hardware purchases, and does not charge a flat fee — billing is based on actual consumption.

quiz.question: An enterprise architect is presenting a cloud strategy where the company will use AWS for customer-facing applications and keep sensitive employee data in their own corporate data center, while also using Azure for backup and archival. Which statement MOST accurately classifies this architecture?
options: This is purely a hybrid cloud architecture because on-premises infrastructure is involved
This is purely a multi-cloud architecture because two public clouds are used
This is both a hybrid and multi-cloud architecture: hybrid due to on-premises infrastructure plus a public cloud, and multi-cloud due to using AWS and Azure
This is a private cloud architecture because sensitive data remains on-premises
quiz.explanation: The architecture is simultaneously hybrid (on-premises corporate data center combined with public cloud) and multi-cloud (AWS and Azure are two separate public cloud providers). These are complementary classifications that can both apply. Calling it purely one or the other would be incomplete. On-premises storage of sensitive data does not make the overall architecture a private cloud.

lessons.title: AWS Global Infrastructure
lessons.overview: Understand how AWS organizes its global infrastructure: Data Centers, Availability Zones, Regions, and Edge Locations — the building blocks for designing highly available and fault-tolerant architectures.
lessons.overviewVi: Tìm hiểu cách AWS tổ chức hạ tầng toàn cầu: Data Center, Availability Zone, Region và Edge Location — các thành phần cơ bản để thiết kế kiến trúc có tính sẵn sàng cao và khả năng chịu lỗi.

sections.label: Overview
sections.heading: The 3-Tier AWS Infrastructure
sections.contentHtml:
AWS provides the most extensive global cloud infrastructure of any cloud provider. It serves over 1 million active customers in 190+ countries. The global infrastructure is organized into three main components:

Data CenterPhysical facility housing hundreds of servers, networking devices, and storage appliances
Availability Zone (AZ)Cluster of one or more data centers with independent power, networking, and connectivity; DCs within 100 km of each other
RegionGroup of multiple AZs within the same geographic area (same country)
Edge Location / POPCDN cache point — delivers content with low latency to global end users
Deploy across multiple AZs and Regions to achieve both High Availability and Disaster Recovery.
sections.contentVi: AWS có hạ tầng đám mây toàn cầu lớn nhất trong các nhà cung cấp đám mây, phục vụ hơn 1 triệu khách hàng tại hơn 190 quốc gia. Hạ tầng toàn cầu được tổ chức thành bốn thành phần chính: Data Center (cơ sở vật lý chứa hàng trăm máy chủ, thiết bị mạng và lưu trữ), Availability Zone hay AZ (cụm một hoặc nhiều data center với nguồn điện và kết nối mạng độc lập; các DC trong AZ cách nhau dưới 100 km), Region (nhóm nhiều AZ trong cùng một quốc gia), Edge Location hay Point of Presence (điểm cache CDN phân phối nội dung với độ trễ thấp đến người dùng cuối toàn cầu). Triển khai trên nhiều AZ và Region giúp đạt được High Availability và Disaster Recovery.

sections.label: Core Concept
sections.heading: Availability Zone (AZ)
sections.contentHtml:
An Availability Zone (AZ) consists of one or more physically separate data centers, each with:

  • Resident power, networking, and connectivity
  • Data centers within 100 km (60 miles) of each other
  • Completely independent power and network connections
  • A failure in one AZ does NOT affect other AZs
Deploying across 2 or more AZs achieves High Availability (HA) — the baseline recommended architecture for production workloads.

Since a region has multiple AZs, your system can remain running even if three or more data centers experience simultaneous outages.
sections.contentVi: Một Availability Zone gồm một hoặc nhiều data center vật lý riêng biệt, mỗi data center có: nguồn điện, mạng và kết nối hoàn toàn độc lập; các data center trong cùng AZ cách nhau trong phạm vi 100 km (60 dặm); hệ thống điện và mạng hoàn toàn tách biệt. Sự cố ở một AZ KHÔNG ảnh hưởng đến các AZ khác. Triển khai trên 2 AZ trở lên giúp đạt được High Availability (HA) — kiến trúc baseline được khuyến nghị cho workload production. Vì mỗi Region có nhiều AZ, hệ thống của bạn vẫn tiếp tục chạy dù ba data center trở lên bị sự cố đồng thời.

sections.label: Core Concept
sections.heading: AWS Regions
sections.contentHtml:
An AWS Region consists of multiple Availability Zones. Regions span North America, South America, Europe, Asia Pacific, and the Middle East.

  • Each Region contains 2 or more AZs
  • AZs within a Region are separated by tens to hundreds of kilometers
  • All AZs remain within the same country to comply with data sovereignty laws
Data Sovereignty: Laws in some countries require data to remain within national borders. AWS ensures AZs within a Region stay in the same country to meet these compliance requirements.

Multi-Region = Disaster Recovery

  • Replicating data to 2+ Regions protects against region-wide disasters (power outages, natural events)
  • Multi-AZ ≠ Multi-Region: Multi-AZ = High Availability; Multi-Region = Disaster Recovery
sections.contentVi: Một AWS Region bao gồm nhiều Availability Zone trong cùng một quốc gia, đảm bảo tuân thủ các luật về chủ quyền dữ liệu — mỗi Region có từ 2 AZ trở lên, các AZ cách nhau hàng chục đến hàng trăm km. Chủ quyền dữ liệu: luật nhiều quốc gia yêu cầu dữ liệu phải nằm trong lãnh thổ — AWS giữ các AZ trong cùng quốc gia để đáp ứng yêu cầu tuân thủ. Multi-Region = Disaster Recovery: sao chép dữ liệu sang 2+ Region khác nhau bảo vệ trước thảm họa ảnh hưởng toàn Region (mất điện diện rộng, thiên tai). Phân biệt quan trọng: Multi-AZ = High Availability; Multi-Region = Disaster Recovery — hai mục đích hoàn toàn khác nhau.

sections.label: Deep Dive
sections.heading: Edge Location / Point of Presence (POP)
sections.contentHtml:
The Edge Network (Point of Presence) consists of Edge Locations and Regional Edge Caches. These enable distributing content with low latency to global end users.

Example: High-resolution images stored on a server in California can be cached at an Edge Location in Singapore. Users in Vietnam retrieve the images from Singapore instead of California — dramatically faster load times.
  • A POP acts as an access point that allows two different networks to communicate
  • Cached content is served from the nearest Edge Location, not the origin server
  • This is the mechanism behind Amazon CloudFront (CDN)
  • Also used by: Route 53, Lambda@Edge, AWS Shield
Edge Location ≠ AZ ≠ Region — these are three completely different infrastructure concepts. A common exam trap!
sections.contentVi: Edge Network (Point of Presence) gồm Edge Location và Regional Edge Cache — cho phép phân phối nội dung với độ trễ thấp đến người dùng cuối toàn cầu. Ví dụ: hình ảnh độ phân giải cao lưu trên máy chủ tại California được cache tại Edge Location ở Singapore — người dùng Việt Nam tải hình từ Singapore thay vì California, nhanh hơn nhiều. POP hoạt động như điểm truy cập cho phép hai mạng khác nhau giao tiếp. Nội dung được phục vụ từ Edge Location gần nhất, không phải từ máy chủ gốc. Đây là cơ chế hoạt động của Amazon CloudFront (CDN). Các dịch vụ khác cũng dùng Edge Location: Route 53, Lambda@Edge, AWS Shield. Lưu ý quan trọng: Edge Location ≠ AZ ≠ Region — đây là ba khái niệm hạ tầng hoàn toàn khác nhau, là bẫy thi phổ biến.

sections.label: Exam Focus
sections.heading: Common Mistakes and Key Distinctions
sections.contentHtml:
Infrastructure Hierarchy (small to large)

  • 1. Data Center — physical building
  • 2. Availability Zone — cluster of DCs, ≤100 km between DCs
  • 3. Region — multiple AZs within one country
  • 4. Edge Location — separate CDN cache point (not part of the AZ/Region hierarchy)

Key Distinctions

  • Edge Location is NOT an AZ
  • Multi-AZ ≠ Multi-Region (different goals)
  • Deploy across 2+ AZs = High Availability
  • Replicate across 2+ Regions = Disaster Recovery
  • Data sovereignty → AZs stay within the same country
sections.contentVi: Thứ tự phân cấp hạ tầng từ nhỏ đến lớn: (1) Data Center — tòa nhà vật lý, (2) Availability Zone — cụm DC, khoảng cách ≤100 km, (3) Region — nhiều AZ trong cùng một quốc gia, (4) Edge Location — điểm cache CDN riêng biệt, không nằm trong phân cấp AZ/Region. Các điểm phân biệt quan trọng: Edge Location KHÔNG phải AZ; Multi-AZ ≠ Multi-Region (mục đích khác nhau); triển khai 2+ AZ = High Availability (HA); sao chép dữ liệu sang 2+ Region = Disaster Recovery (DR); chủ quyền dữ liệu = lý do các AZ luôn nằm trong cùng quốc gia.

summary: AWS infrastructure hierarchy: Data Center → AZ (DCs within 100 km) → Region (multiple AZs, same country) → Edge Location (CDN cache)
Deploy across 2+ AZs = High Availability | Replicate across 2+ Regions = Disaster Recovery
Edge Location / POP = CDN cache point used by CloudFront — NOT an AZ or Region
Data sovereignty: AWS keeps AZs within the same country to comply with data storage laws
summaryVi: Phân cấp hạ tầng AWS: Data Center → AZ (các DC trong phạm vi 100 km) → Region (nhiều AZ, cùng quốc gia) → Edge Location (cache CDN)
Triển khai 2+ AZ = High Availability | Sao chép sang 2+ Region = Disaster Recovery
Edge Location / POP = điểm cache CDN của CloudFront — KHÔNG phải AZ hay Region
Chủ quyền dữ liệu: AWS giữ các AZ trong cùng quốc gia để tuân thủ luật lưu trữ dữ liệu

quiz.question: A startup is deploying its first production application on AWS. The solutions architect wants to ensure the application remains available if a single data center experiences a power outage. What is the MINIMUM deployment strategy to achieve this?
options: Deploy the application in a single Availability Zone with Auto Scaling
Deploy the application across two Availability Zones within the same Region
Deploy the application across two different AWS Regions
Deploy the application behind an Edge Location using CloudFront
quiz.explanation: Deploying across two or more Availability Zones within the same Region provides High Availability against a single data center or AZ failure. Each AZ consists of one or more discrete data centers, so a failure in one AZ does not affect another. A single AZ (even with Auto Scaling) cannot survive an AZ-level outage, multi-Region is Disaster Recovery (beyond the minimum needed), and Edge Locations are CDN cache points — not compute deployment targets.

quiz.question: A financial services company stores sensitive customer data on AWS. A new regulation requires that all customer data must remain within the country's borders at all times. The solutions architect is evaluating whether AWS can comply with this requirement. Which AWS infrastructure design principle directly supports this compliance need?
options: Edge Locations cache data globally, ensuring low latency without moving source data
AWS Regions are designed so that all Availability Zones within a Region are located within the same country
AWS replicates data automatically across Regions, but only within the same continent
Availability Zones are isolated buildings, ensuring data never leaves a single facility
quiz.explanation: AWS deliberately keeps all Availability Zones within a Region inside the same country to help customers comply with data sovereignty and data residency laws. Data does NOT automatically replicate across Regions — that requires explicit customer action. Edge Locations are global CDN cache points that are separate from Regions and do not store source data.

quiz.question: A media company streams video content to users across North America and Europe. Users in Europe are experiencing high latency when accessing content stored in the us-east-1 Region. What AWS infrastructure component should the solutions architect leverage to reduce latency for European users with the LEAST operational overhead?
options: Launch EC2 instances in a European Region and replicate content there
Create an additional Availability Zone in us-east-1 closer to Europe
Use CloudFront Edge Locations to cache and serve content closer to European users
Move all application infrastructure to a European Region
quiz.explanation: CloudFront Edge Locations (Points of Presence) are CDN cache points distributed globally that serve cached content to end users with low latency. This requires no changes to the origin infrastructure and involves the least operational overhead. Launching instances in a new Region or migrating the entire stack introduces significant operational complexity. Availability Zones cannot be created on demand or placed across oceans.

quiz.question: A healthcare company needs its AWS architecture to survive a complete regional disaster, such as a hurricane destroying an entire AWS Region. Which TWO architectural decisions should the solutions architect implement? (Choose TWO)
options: Deploy application servers across multiple Availability Zones within the primary Region
Replicate critical data to at least one additional AWS Region
Use CloudFront Edge Locations as failover compute environments
Deploy the application stack in a second AWS Region
Increase the number of data centers within the primary Availability Zone
quiz.explanation: Surviving a full regional disaster requires data replication to another Region and active (or standby) infrastructure in a second Region — this is the definition of Disaster Recovery. Multi-AZ deployment (option A) provides High Availability within a Region but cannot survive the entire Region failing. Edge Locations are CDN cache points, not compute environments, and cannot host application logic. The number of data centers in an AZ is managed by AWS, not the customer.

quiz.question: An e-commerce company's application runs in a single AWS Region. During a recent incident, one Availability Zone became unavailable, causing a 45-minute outage. The solutions architect must redesign the architecture to prevent future outages from a single AZ failure while keeping costs MOST cost-effective. What should the architect recommend?
options: Migrate the entire workload to a larger instance type in the same AZ to reduce failure risk
Deploy the application across all Availability Zones in two different Regions with active-active configuration
Deploy the application across at least two Availability Zones within the same Region using a load balancer
Enable CloudFront in front of the application to absorb traffic during AZ failures
quiz.explanation: Deploying across two or more AZs within the same Region is the standard, cost-effective pattern for High Availability — a load balancer distributes traffic and can route away from a failed AZ automatically. Using a larger instance in one AZ does not eliminate the AZ failure risk. A multi-Region active-active setup is far more expensive and exceeds the requirement. CloudFront caches static content but cannot replace application compute during an AZ outage.

quiz.question: A solutions architect is explaining AWS infrastructure to a new team member. The team member asks why AWS places multiple data centers close together and treats them as a single Availability Zone rather than separate zones. What is the PRIMARY reason for this design?
options: It reduces the number of IP address ranges AWS must manage globally
It allows workloads to span multiple physical facilities while still maintaining low-latency, synchronous replication between them
It ensures that a single natural disaster can never affect more than one data center
It simplifies AWS billing by grouping data centers into a single chargeable unit
quiz.explanation: An AZ consists of one or more discrete data centers within approximately 100 km of each other, enabling synchronous replication with sub-millisecond latency between facilities while presenting a single logical deployment target. This grouping also provides redundancy within the AZ itself. The proximity limit (100 km) actually means a large-scale disaster could affect multiple data centers in one AZ — high-availability across AZs mitigates broader failures.

quiz.question: A company is reviewing its AWS architecture and notices that its static website assets are served directly from an S3 bucket in ap-southeast-1. Users in the United States complain about slow load times. The operations team wants a solution with LEAST operational overhead. What should the solutions architect recommend?
options: Create a second S3 bucket in us-east-1 and manually sync assets between the two buckets
Launch EC2 web servers in us-east-1 to serve content closer to US users
Configure Amazon CloudFront with the S3 bucket as the origin to cache assets at Edge Locations near US users
Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on the bucket to speed up downloads for US users
quiz.explanation: CloudFront distributes content through its global network of Edge Locations (Points of Presence), caching assets at locations near end users. This requires no server management and minimal configuration, making it the lowest operational overhead option. Manual S3 sync requires ongoing maintenance. EC2 servers add significant operational overhead. S3 Transfer Acceleration improves upload speeds, not download speeds for website visitors.

quiz.question: A solutions architect is documenting the differences between AWS Availability Zones and Edge Locations for a junior engineer. Which TWO statements accurately describe the distinction between these infrastructure components? (Choose TWO)
options: Availability Zones can host EC2 instances and RDS databases; Edge Locations cannot
Edge Locations are part of an AWS Region and appear in the Region's AZ count
Edge Locations serve as CDN cache points for services like CloudFront, not as general compute environments
Availability Zones and Edge Locations both count toward a Region's redundancy for disaster recovery
There are significantly more Edge Locations globally than there are Availability Zones
quiz.explanation: AZs are the building blocks for deploying compute, storage, and databases; EC2 and RDS run in AZs, not Edge Locations. Edge Locations (Points of Presence) are solely used for CDN caching (CloudFront) and are not general-purpose compute environments. Edge Locations are NOT part of a Region or AZ — they are a separate layer of infrastructure. There are indeed more Edge Locations than AZs globally, but that fact (option E) is trivia, while option A and C describe architectural behaviors directly relevant to design decisions.

quiz.question: A retail company operates in Canada and is subject to Canadian data residency laws. The CTO asks whether using AWS CloudFront Edge Locations will cause Canadian customer data to be stored outside Canada. How should the solutions architect respond?
options: Yes — CloudFront Edge Locations are global and will cache customer PII outside Canada
No — Edge Locations only cache content defined by the architect; sensitive data that is not served through CloudFront stays in the origin Region
Yes — all CloudFront distributions automatically replicate origin data to all global Edge Locations
No — CloudFront Edge Locations are exclusively located within the customer's home Region
quiz.explanation: Edge Locations cache only the content routed through CloudFront distributions. Sensitive data stored in databases or S3 buckets that is not served via CloudFront remains in the origin Region (Canada). The architect controls what content is distributed. Edge Locations are NOT confined to a home Region — they are global — but they only cache what they are instructed to serve, not all origin data.

quiz.question: A company's RTO (Recovery Time Objective) is 4 hours and RPO (Recovery Point Objective) is 1 hour in the event of a complete AWS Region failure. Which architectural pattern BEST meets these requirements?
options: Deploy in a single Region across three Availability Zones with automated failover
Use CloudFront Edge Locations to serve traffic and cache data during a Region outage
Replicate data and maintain a warm standby in a second AWS Region, enabling failover within hours
Use S3 Versioning within the primary Region to recover data up to 1 hour prior
quiz.explanation: Surviving a full Region failure requires infrastructure in a second Region. A warm standby keeps a scaled-down copy running in the secondary Region, enabling failover within the RTO of 4 hours and with data replicated within the RPO of 1 hour. Multi-AZ deployment only handles AZ failures, not Region failures. Edge Locations cannot host application compute or serve as a Region failover. S3 Versioning protects against data corruption but does not address Region-level compute failures.

quiz.question: An architect notices that a new AWS Region has only two Availability Zones, while another Region has six. A colleague suggests that having more AZs in a Region always means higher fault tolerance. Is this accurate, and what is the most important factor for fault tolerance within a Region?
options: Correct — more AZs always means higher fault tolerance regardless of deployment configuration
Incorrect — fault tolerance requires deploying across at least two AZs; additional AZs beyond two offer incremental benefit depending on the architecture
Incorrect — fault tolerance is determined by the number of Edge Locations assigned to a Region, not the AZ count
Correct — AWS guarantees 99.99% availability only in Regions with four or more AZs
quiz.explanation: The foundational rule is deploying across 2+ AZs to achieve High Availability. Additional AZs can improve granularity and reduce blast radius further, but the jump from 1 AZ to 2 AZs is the critical threshold. Simply having more AZs provides no benefit if the workload is deployed in only one of them. Edge Location count has no bearing on Regional fault tolerance for compute workloads.

quiz.question: A global logistics company wants to design an AWS architecture that achieves BOTH High Availability and Disaster Recovery for its order management system. Which THREE design decisions should the solutions architect implement? (Choose THREE)
options: Deploy application instances across at least two Availability Zones in the primary Region
Use an Elastic Load Balancer to distribute traffic across multiple AZs in the primary Region
Store all data exclusively in a single AZ to minimize replication lag
Replicate the database to a secondary AWS Region
Deploy a standby version of the application in a secondary AWS Region
quiz.explanation: High Availability is achieved by deploying across 2+ AZs (option A) with a load balancer routing around failed AZs (option B). Disaster Recovery requires cross-Region data replication (option D) and ideally infrastructure in the secondary Region (option E) — however the question asks for THREE choices, and the core DR requirement is replicating data (D). Storing data in a single AZ (option C) is an anti-pattern that eliminates both HA and DR. Option E is also correct DR practice but the three foundational choices are A, B, D.

quiz.question: A solutions architect is selecting an AWS Region for a new workload. The primary requirements are: (1) customer data must stay within Australia, (2) the workload needs High Availability, and (3) latency for Australian users must be minimized. Which combination of AWS infrastructure components should the architect choose?
options: An AWS Region located in Australia, with the application deployed across multiple Availability Zones in that Region
An AWS Region in Asia Pacific with CloudFront Edge Locations in Australia to handle all data storage
Any AWS Region globally, with CloudFront Edge Locations in Australia serving all application data
Multiple AWS Regions worldwide with data replicated to ensure Australian users receive low latency
quiz.explanation: An Australian AWS Region (such as ap-southeast-2 Sydney) keeps data within Australia for sovereignty compliance, and deploying across multiple AZs within that Region satisfies the High Availability requirement while minimizing latency for local users. CloudFront Edge Locations cannot store source data and do not satisfy data residency laws for application databases. Using a non-Australian Region violates the data sovereignty requirement.

quiz.question: During a post-incident review, a team discovers their web application went down because the single Availability Zone they used experienced an infrastructure failure. The team wants to prevent this from happening again. A junior engineer suggests simply using a larger EC2 instance to reduce the risk of hardware failure. Why is this NOT an adequate solution?
options: Larger instances are not available in all Availability Zones
A larger instance still resides in a single AZ and is subject to the same AZ-level failures such as power or network outages
EC2 instance size does not affect availability; only instance type families matter for redundancy
Larger instances automatically replicate across Availability Zones, which duplicates the existing problem
quiz.explanation: An AZ failure (power outage, cooling failure, network disruption) affects all instances in that AZ regardless of their size. A larger instance offers no protection against AZ-level failures. The correct solution is to deploy across multiple AZs. Larger instances do not replicate across AZs — that requires explicit multi-AZ deployment architecture.

quiz.question: A company is conducting a business continuity planning workshop. The solutions architect draws a diagram with four levels of AWS infrastructure: individual data centers, Availability Zones, Regions, and Edge Locations. A stakeholder asks which level is used specifically for CloudFront content delivery caching. What should the architect answer?
options: Regions — CloudFront caches content in Regional edge caches within each Region
Availability Zones — CloudFront deploys cache nodes in each AZ for redundancy
Edge Locations (Points of Presence) — these are the CDN cache points used by CloudFront, distinct from AZs and Regions
Data Centers — CloudFront runs directly within AWS data centers to minimize internal latency
quiz.explanation: Edge Locations (also called Points of Presence or POPs) are the specific infrastructure layer used by CloudFront for CDN caching. They are distinct from Regions and Availability Zones — they are not used for deploying EC2, RDS, or other core AWS services, and they do not count as Regions or AZs. CloudFront does also have Regional edge caches, but the primary cache endpoints facing end users are Edge Locations.

quiz.question: A multinational enterprise is designing its AWS presence. The CTO wants to understand which AWS infrastructure decisions are made by AWS versus which are controlled by the customer. Which TWO statements are accurate? (Choose TWO)
options: Customers decide which Region to deploy workloads in, but AWS controls which country each Region is located in
Customers can create new Availability Zones within an existing Region if they need more capacity
AWS determines the physical locations of data centers within an AZ; customers choose which AZs to deploy into
Customers control whether their data replicates across Regions; AWS does not automatically replicate data across Regions
Edge Location placement is determined by customers based on their end-user geography
quiz.explanation: AWS manages the physical infrastructure — including where data centers and AZs are located — while customers make deployment decisions (which AZ, which Region). Critically, AWS does NOT automatically replicate customer data across Regions; customers must explicitly configure cross-Region replication. Customers cannot create new AZs — these are AWS-managed infrastructure. Edge Location placement is determined by AWS, not customers.

quiz.question: A solutions architect is comparing two failover strategies: (A) using multiple Availability Zones within one Region, and (B) replicating across two Regions. A disaster occurs where an entire AWS Region goes offline. Which statement correctly describes the outcome under each strategy?
options: Both strategies prevent downtime; the multi-AZ strategy is simply cheaper to implement
Strategy A (multi-AZ) maintains availability because AZs are in different countries within the Region
Strategy A (multi-AZ) fails because all AZs are in the same Region; Strategy B (multi-Region) maintains availability by failing over to the second Region
Strategy B (multi-Region) is unnecessary because AWS guarantees 100% Regional uptime by contract
quiz.explanation: Multi-AZ deployment provides High Availability against individual AZ failures but all AZs in a Region share the same Regional infrastructure. If the entire Region fails, a multi-AZ deployment in that Region also fails. Multi-Region replication (Disaster Recovery) maintains availability by allowing failover to a geographically separate Region. AWS does not guarantee 100% Regional uptime; Regions can and do experience large-scale events.

quiz.question: A company wants to serve its global website with the LOWEST possible latency for all users worldwide while keeping the origin infrastructure in a single AWS Region. What is the MOST cost-effective solution?
options: Deploy EC2 instances in every AWS Region and use Route 53 latency-based routing
Place the origin in us-east-1 and rely on users' internet connections to optimize routing naturally
Use Amazon CloudFront with Edge Locations globally to cache and deliver website content close to users
Replicate the origin S3 bucket to every AWS Region using cross-Region replication
quiz.explanation: CloudFront leverages hundreds of Edge Locations globally to cache content close to end users, dramatically reducing latency without the cost of maintaining infrastructure in every Region. Deploying EC2 in every Region is extremely expensive and operationally complex. Relying on the public internet provides no latency optimization. Cross-Region S3 replication does not put compute or CDN logic near users and requires managing infrastructure in every Region.

quiz.question: A startup architect is told that Availability Zones within a Region are placed within 100 km of each other. A colleague argues this proximity means AZs offer no real isolation since a large earthquake could destroy all of them simultaneously. How should the architect address this concern when designing for High Availability?
options: The colleague is correct; true HA requires deploying in Regions on different continents

Multi-AZ deployment provides High Availability against the most common failure types (hardware, power, network); for protection against large-scale regional disasters, multi-Region Disaster Recovery should be added as an additional layer
The 100 km distance is sufficient to protect against all known natural disasters, so multi-AZ alone covers every failure scenario
AZs are always 500 km apart, so the colleague's concern is based on incorrect information
quiz.explanation: Multi-AZ addresses the vast majority of failure scenarios (hardware failure, localized power outages, network issues) and is the standard pattern for High Availability. For protection against large-scale regional disasters (earthquakes, hurricanes), multi-Region Disaster Recovery is the additional layer. AWS's model explicitly separates High Availability (multi-AZ) from Disaster Recovery (multi-Region) — both are valid and complementary strategies rather than one replacing the other.

quiz.question: A solutions architect is reviewing an architecture that currently has all resources deployed in a single Availability Zone. The team wants to improve the design for a critical application. Which TWO changes would move the architecture from a single point of failure to a Highly Available design? (Choose TWO)
options: Add an Application Load Balancer configured to route traffic across multiple Availability Zones
Deploy application instances in at least one additional Availability Zone in the same Region
Enable CloudFront in front of the application to cache responses at Edge Locations
Move the application to a Region with more Availability Zones
Increase EC2 instance sizes to handle more traffic if one instance fails
quiz.explanation: High Availability requires both deploying resources across multiple AZs (option B) and having a mechanism to route traffic away from the failed AZ (option A — a load balancer). These two changes together implement the standard multi-AZ HA pattern. CloudFront improves latency and caches static content but does not make the origin application highly available. Simply moving to a Region with more AZs does not help unless the application is also deployed across those AZs. Larger instances do not protect against AZ-level failures.

lessons.title: AWS Shared Responsibility
lessons.overview: The Shared Responsibility Model is one of the most heavily tested topics on the SAA exam. Learn exactly what AWS is responsible for versus what the customer must manage.
lessons.overviewVi: Mô hình Shared Responsibility là một trong những chủ đề được kiểm tra nhiều nhất trong kỳ thi SAA. Hiểu rõ AWS chịu trách nhiệm gì so với những gì khách hàng phải quản lý.

sections.label: Overview
sections.heading: What is the Shared Responsibility Model?
sections.contentHtml:
The AWS Shared Responsibility Model defines exactly which security and compliance tasks belong to AWS and which belong to the customer. The key phrase to remember:

AWSSecurity OF the cloud — protects the physical infrastructure that runs all AWS services
CustomerSecurity IN the cloud — protects data, configurations, and applications they run on AWS
Exam trap: "OF" and "IN" look nearly identical but mean completely different things. Memorize this distinction — it appears in nearly every version of the exam.

The model also defines three types of IT controls: inherited, shared, and customer-specific.
sections.contentVi: Mô hình AWS Shared Responsibility xác định rõ ràng nhiệm vụ bảo mật và tuân thủ thuộc về AWS và nhiệm vụ nào thuộc về khách hàng. Cụm từ cần ghi nhớ: AWS chịu trách nhiệm bảo mật 'OF' (CỦA) đám mây — bảo vệ toàn bộ hạ tầng vật lý chạy tất cả dịch vụ AWS. Khách hàng chịu trách nhiệm bảo mật 'IN' (TRONG) đám mây — bảo vệ dữ liệu, cấu hình và ứng dụng họ chạy trên AWS. Bẫy thi: 'OF' và 'IN' trông giống nhau nhưng có ý nghĩa hoàn toàn khác nhau — hãy ghi nhớ sự phân biệt này vì nó xuất hiện trong hầu hết mọi phiên bản đề thi. Mô hình còn định nghĩa 3 loại IT controls: inherited (thừa hưởng từ AWS), shared (chia sẻ hai bên), và customer-specific (riêng của khách hàng).

sections.label: AWS Responsibilities
sections.heading: Security OF the Cloud (AWS)
sections.contentHtml:
AWS is responsible for protecting the entire physical infrastructure that powers the AWS Cloud — everything the customer cannot touch or see:

  • Physical security of all data centers (guards, cameras, access controls)
  • All hardware: servers, networking devices, storage appliances
  • The Host Operating System and Hypervisor (virtualization layer)
  • Firmware updates on computing and networking hardware
  • Availability, reliability, and scalability of cloud services

For Abstracted Services (S3, DynamoDB, Lambda)

  • AWS also manages: OS, runtime, platform, and all underlying software layers
  • The customer has no visibility into or control over these layers
The more abstracted the service, the more AWS takes responsibility for. Lambda = AWS manages everything except your function code.
sections.contentVi: AWS chịu trách nhiệm bảo vệ toàn bộ hạ tầng vật lý vận hành AWS Cloud — tất cả những gì khách hàng không thể chạm hoặc nhìn thấy: bảo mật vật lý tất cả data center (bảo vệ, camera, kiểm soát ra vào), phần cứng (máy chủ, thiết bị mạng, thiết bị lưu trữ), Host Operating System và Hypervisor (lớp ảo hóa), cập nhật firmware cho phần cứng tính toán và mạng, tính sẵn sàng và độ tin cậy của dịch vụ đám mây. Với các dịch vụ trừu tượng (S3, DynamoDB, Lambda): AWS còn quản lý thêm OS, runtime, platform và tất cả các lớp phần mềm bên dưới — khách hàng không có quyền nhìn hoặc kiểm soát các lớp này. Dịch vụ càng trừu tượng, AWS càng chịu nhiều trách nhiệm — với Lambda, AWS quản lý tất cả trừ code hàm của bạn.

sections.label: Customer Responsibilities
sections.heading: Security IN the Cloud (Customer)
sections.contentHtml:
For Amazon EC2 (IaaS — most customer responsibility)

  • Patching the Guest OS (Linux or Windows inside the EC2 instance)
  • Configuring Security Groups (virtual firewall) and Network ACLs
  • Managing IAM — users, roles, policies, access keys, and secret keys
  • Enabling and managing data encryption (client-side and server-side)
  • Zone security — controlling network routing to prevent unauthorized access

For Abstracted Services (S3, DynamoDB)

  • Configuring IAM policies and bucket/resource policies
  • Enabling encryption at rest and in transit
  • Configuring endpoints and fine-grained access permissions
Rule of thumb: EC2 (IaaS) = customer has the most responsibility. S3/Lambda (abstracted) = AWS handles the most.
sections.contentVi: Khách hàng chịu trách nhiệm bảo mật những gì họ đặt và kiểm soát trong đám mây. Với Amazon EC2 (IaaS — nhiều trách nhiệm khách hàng nhất): vá lỗi Guest OS (Linux hoặc Windows bên trong EC2), cấu hình Security Groups (tường lửa ảo) và Network ACL, quản lý IAM (users, roles, policies, access keys và secret keys), bật và quản lý mã hóa dữ liệu (phía client và phía server), kiểm soát zone security để ngăn truy cập trái phép. Với dịch vụ trừu tượng (S3, DynamoDB): cấu hình IAM policies và bucket/resource policies, bật mã hóa khi lưu trữ và khi truyền tải, cấu hình endpoints và quyền truy cập chi tiết. Quy tắc nhanh: EC2 (IaaS) = khách hàng chịu trách nhiệm nhiều nhất; S3/Lambda (trừu tượng) = AWS xử lý nhiều nhất.

sections.label: Quick Reference
sections.heading: Who Is Responsible for What?
sections.contentHtml:
AWS Only

Physical data center securityHost OS patchingHypervisorHardware firmwareLambda server infrastructure

Customer Only

Guest OS patching (EC2)Security GroupsNetwork ACLsIAM users and access keysData encryptionZone security routing

Shared Controls

Patch managementConfiguration managementAwareness and training
Shared patch management: AWS patches Host OS; customer patches Guest OS and applications.
sections.contentVi: Chỉ AWS chịu trách nhiệm: bảo mật vật lý data center, vá lỗi Host OS, Hypervisor, firmware phần cứng, hạ tầng máy chủ Lambda. Chỉ khách hàng chịu trách nhiệm: vá lỗi Guest OS (EC2), Security Groups, Network ACL, IAM users và access keys, mã hóa dữ liệu, định tuyến zone security. Shared controls (chia sẻ hai bên): Patch management — AWS vá Host OS, khách hàng vá Guest OS và ứng dụng; Configuration management — AWS quản lý hạ tầng, khách hàng quản lý ứng dụng; Awareness and training — cả hai bên đều có trách nhiệm đào tạo nhân sự của mình.

summary: AWS = Security OF the cloud: physical infrastructure, host OS, hypervisor, data center physical security
Customer = Security IN the cloud: guest OS, security groups, IAM, data encryption, zone security
EC2 (IaaS): customer bears the most responsibility | S3/DynamoDB (abstracted): AWS handles most layers
Three IT control types: Inherited (from AWS) | Shared (both parties) | Customer-specific
summaryVi: AWS = Bảo mật CỦA đám mây: hạ tầng vật lý, Host OS, hypervisor, bảo mật vật lý data center
Khách hàng = Bảo mật TRONG đám mây: Guest OS, security groups, IAM, mã hóa dữ liệu, zone security
EC2 (IaaS): khách hàng chịu nhiều trách nhiệm nhất | S3/DynamoDB (trừu tượng): AWS xử lý hầu hết
Ba loại IT control: Inherited (thừa hưởng từ AWS) | Shared (chia sẻ) | Customer-specific (riêng khách hàng)

quiz.question: A company is migrating its on-premises application to AWS and wants to understand which security tasks it will no longer be responsible for. Which responsibility shifts entirely to AWS after migration to EC2?
options: Patching the guest operating system on EC2 instances
Managing IAM user permissions for application access
Maintaining the physical security of the data center
Configuring security groups for inbound traffic
quiz.explanation: Physical data center security (locks, guards, surveillance) is entirely AWS's responsibility under the Shared Responsibility Model — this is 'Security OF the cloud.' Guest OS patching, IAM management, and security group configuration all remain the customer's responsibility when using EC2 (IaaS).

quiz.question: A startup stores sensitive customer records in Amazon S3. A security audit finds that objects are publicly accessible. According to the AWS Shared Responsibility Model, who is responsible for fixing this misconfiguration?
options: AWS, because S3 is a managed service and AWS controls its configuration
The customer, because bucket policies and access controls are customer-managed
AWS, because data encryption at rest is an AWS-managed control
Both AWS and the customer share equal responsibility for S3 bucket policies
quiz.explanation: Even though S3 is a managed (abstracted) service where AWS handles the underlying infrastructure, bucket policies, ACLs, and Block Public Access settings are customer-configured. Misconfiguring access controls is a customer responsibility — 'Security IN the cloud.' AWS provides the tools; the customer must use them correctly.

quiz.question: An enterprise runs a fleet of EC2 instances and also uses DynamoDB for session storage. Compared to DynamoDB, which additional security responsibility does the customer take on specifically because of using EC2?
options: Encrypting data at rest within the storage service
Patching and maintaining the guest operating system
Ensuring the physical host hardware is not compromised
Managing the underlying hypervisor security patches
quiz.explanation: EC2 is IaaS, so the customer is responsible for the guest OS — including patches and configuration. With DynamoDB (a fully managed service), AWS handles the OS, patching, and infrastructure layers. Physical hardware and hypervisor security are always AWS's responsibility regardless of service type.

quiz.question: A financial services company is performing a compliance review of its AWS environment. The auditor asks which controls are the customer's sole responsibility when running applications on EC2. Which TWO items are customer-specific controls? (Choose TWO)
options: Hypervisor patch management
IAM policies governing developer access
Physical host decommissioning procedures
Security group rules for the EC2 instances
Data center environmental controls (power, cooling)
quiz.explanation: IAM policies and security group rules are customer-specific controls — AWS provides these tools but the customer defines and manages them. Hypervisor patching, physical decommissioning, and data center environmental controls (power, cooling) are all inherited controls fully managed by AWS.

quiz.question: A solutions architect is documenting controls for a SOC 2 audit. The auditor classifies some AWS controls as 'inherited.' Which control best fits the 'inherited from AWS' category?
options: Enabling MFA on IAM user accounts
Encrypting S3 objects using SSE-S3
Physical access controls to AWS data centers
Defining VPC network ACL rules
quiz.explanation: Inherited controls are those fully managed by AWS that customers can rely on without any action — physical access controls to data centers are a prime example. MFA enrollment, S3 encryption configuration, and VPC ACL rules all require customer action and are therefore customer-specific or shared controls.

quiz.question: A company wants to use Amazon RDS instead of managing a self-hosted MySQL database on EC2. Which responsibility does the company give up by switching from MySQL on EC2 to Amazon RDS?
options: Defining which IAM roles can access the database
Managing database user accounts and passwords
Patching the underlying database engine software
Enabling encryption for data in transit to the database
quiz.explanation: With RDS (a managed service), AWS takes on database engine patching, which was the customer's job when running MySQL on EC2. IAM role definitions, database user management, and enabling in-transit encryption are still customer responsibilities regardless of whether RDS or self-managed MySQL is used.

quiz.question: A healthcare company is mapping AWS Shared Responsibility controls ahead of a HIPAA audit. Which TWO of the following are examples of SHARED controls — responsibilities that apply to both AWS and the customer, each in their own context? (Choose TWO)
options: Patch management (AWS patches infrastructure; customer patches guest OS)
Physical perimeter security of AWS data centers
Configuration management (AWS configures its infrastructure; customer configures their resources)
Customer data classification and labeling
Hypervisor vulnerability remediation
quiz.explanation: Patch management and configuration management are classic shared controls — AWS and the customer each apply them to their respective layers. Physical perimeter security and hypervisor remediation are inherited (AWS-only) controls. Data classification and labeling is a customer-specific control.

quiz.question: An architect is explaining to a new team member why using DynamoDB reduces their security workload compared to running a NoSQL engine on EC2. Which statement MOST accurately describes the difference in customer responsibility?
options: DynamoDB eliminates all customer security responsibilities because AWS manages the entire service
With DynamoDB, the customer no longer manages the OS, storage, or network infrastructure layers
With DynamoDB, the customer is still responsible for patching the underlying host servers
EC2 and DynamoDB have identical customer security responsibilities because both run on AWS infrastructure
quiz.explanation: DynamoDB is a fully abstracted managed service — AWS handles the OS, storage, and network layers, significantly reducing customer responsibility. However, customers still control IAM access, data encryption settings, and VPC endpoint configurations. Option A is incorrect because customers always retain some responsibilities (e.g., IAM, data).

quiz.question: During a security incident, it is discovered that an EC2 instance was compromised through an unpatched vulnerability in the instance's operating system. Under the AWS Shared Responsibility Model, which party failed in their duty?
options: AWS, because they are responsible for securing all compute infrastructure
The customer, because guest OS patching on EC2 is a customer responsibility
AWS, because hypervisor vulnerabilities allowed the OS to be compromised
Both parties equally, because EC2 is a jointly managed service
quiz.explanation: The guest OS on EC2 is entirely the customer's responsibility to patch and maintain. AWS is responsible for the hypervisor and underlying hardware, not the OS running on top. Since the vulnerability was in the instance OS — not the hypervisor — the customer bears responsibility for this failure.

quiz.question: A company uses AWS Lambda for its backend API. A security engineer asks who is responsible for patching the runtime environment (e.g., Node.js runtime). What is the correct answer according to the Shared Responsibility Model?
options: The customer, because they choose and configure the Lambda runtime
AWS, because Lambda is a managed service and AWS maintains the execution environment
The customer, because runtime selection is a customer-specific control
Both AWS and the customer share equal responsibility for Lambda runtime patches
quiz.explanation: Lambda is a serverless, fully managed service where AWS owns the execution environment including runtime patching and OS maintenance. This is analogous to DynamoDB — the more abstracted the service, the more infrastructure layers AWS manages. The customer is responsible for function code security and IAM permissions.

quiz.question: A compliance officer needs to identify which security controls are the CUSTOMER'S responsibility when deploying an application on Amazon EC2. Which THREE of the following are customer responsibilities? (Choose THREE)
options: Configuring inbound and outbound security group rules
Patching the physical host server firmware
Managing encryption of data stored on EBS volumes
Ensuring the AWS global network backbone is secure
Creating and enforcing IAM policies for application access
quiz.explanation: Security group configuration, EBS encryption management, and IAM policy enforcement are all customer responsibilities on EC2 (IaaS). Physical host firmware and the global network backbone are AWS's infrastructure responsibilities — these are inherited controls the customer does not manage.

quiz.question: A solutions architect is advising a team choosing between hosting a PostgreSQL database on EC2 versus using Amazon Aurora. From a Shared Responsibility perspective, which statement correctly describes the impact of choosing Aurora?
options: Aurora removes all security responsibilities from the customer since it is fully managed
Aurora shifts OS patching and database engine maintenance to AWS, reducing customer operational burden
Aurora increases customer responsibility because managed services require more security configuration
There is no difference in security responsibility between EC2-hosted PostgreSQL and Aurora
quiz.explanation: Aurora (managed service) shifts OS and database engine patching to AWS, reducing operational overhead for the customer. However, customers retain responsibility for IAM, network security, and data encryption configuration. Option A is wrong because customers always have 'Security IN the cloud' responsibilities.

quiz.question: An organization's AWS environment undergoes a physical security audit. The auditor wants to visit the AWS data center to inspect physical controls. What should the AWS account manager tell them?
options: Customers can schedule data center visits after signing an NDA
Physical data center security is AWS's responsibility; AWS provides audit reports such as SOC 2 instead
Physical security inspection rights are included in the AWS Business Support plan
Customers share responsibility for physical security and can request partial access
quiz.explanation: Physical data center security is 100% AWS's responsibility — an inherited control. Customers cannot visit or inspect AWS facilities, but AWS provides third-party audit reports (SOC 1/2/3, ISO 27001, etc.) as evidence of physical controls. This is a fundamental aspect of the Shared Responsibility Model.

quiz.question: A team migrates from on-premises servers to EC2. The security team is worried about zone-level security — specifically about workload isolation between their EC2 instances and other customers' instances on the same physical host. Which party is responsible for this isolation?
options: The customer, by configuring dedicated host placement groups
The customer, by enabling EC2 instance metadata service restrictions
AWS, through hypervisor-enforced isolation between tenants
Both AWS and the customer share responsibility for multi-tenant isolation
quiz.explanation: Hypervisor-level tenant isolation — preventing one customer's EC2 instance from accessing another's memory or storage on the same physical host — is AWS's responsibility. This is 'Security OF the cloud.' Dedicated Hosts are a customer option for compliance, but baseline multi-tenant isolation is always enforced by AWS.

quiz.question: A developer argues that because AWS manages the S3 infrastructure, the team does not need to worry about enabling server-side encryption for their S3 buckets storing PII. How should a solutions architect respond?
options: The developer is correct — AWS encrypts all S3 data by default and the customer has no further obligation
The developer is incorrect — choosing and configuring encryption for customer data in S3 is a customer responsibility
The developer is correct — S3 is a fully managed service so all data protection is AWS's responsibility
The developer is partially correct — AWS encrypts data in transit but the customer must handle encryption at rest
quiz.explanation: Data encryption — deciding whether and how to encrypt customer data — is a customer responsibility regardless of service abstraction level. While AWS may offer default encryption (SSE-S3), the customer is responsible for ensuring appropriate encryption is configured for sensitive data. This falls under 'Security IN the cloud.'

quiz.question: An enterprise architect is building a responsibility matrix for a new AWS deployment. They need to classify several controls. Which TWO controls should be classified as INHERITED (fully owned by AWS)? (Choose TWO)
options: Network ACL configuration for the customer's VPC
Decommissioning and disposal of physical storage media
Availability Zone power and cooling infrastructure
S3 bucket versioning configuration
EC2 instance security group egress rules
quiz.explanation: Physical storage media disposal and AZ power/cooling infrastructure are inherited controls — the customer has zero involvement or visibility into these AWS-managed physical operations. VPC network ACLs, S3 versioning, and EC2 security group rules are all customer-configured controls.

quiz.question: A security-conscious company wants to ensure their EC2 workloads are isolated at the hardware level from other AWS customers for regulatory compliance. They ask whose responsibility it is to guarantee this isolation is in place. What is the correct answer?
options: The customer must configure VPC isolation to achieve hardware-level separation
AWS guarantees hypervisor-based isolation for all EC2 instances by default; the customer can optionally use Dedicated Hosts for additional compliance evidence
